title: Greedy choice and counterexamples

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Greedy choice and counterexamples; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Greedy choice and counterexamples
sources.claim: A greedy algorithm makes a locally optimal safe choice and needs an exchange, cut, or staying-ahead argument for global correctness.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A greedy algorithm makes a locally optimal safe choice and needs an exchange, cut, or staying-ahead argument for global correctness.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Interval scheduling selects the compatible interval with earliest finish and proves an exchange with an optimal solution. Your goal is to design a greedy solution, state its ordering and produce a counterexample when the property fails. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Recursion and divide-and-conquer; Hash tables, sets and maps; Correctness and counterexamples; Sorting properties and selection. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A greedy algorithm makes a locally optimal safe choice and needs an exchange, cut, or staying-ahead argument for global correctness. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: Across C, C++, Java, and Python, keep the state transition and invariant language-neutral first; then map indexes, numeric overflow, recursion, mutable collections, hashing, and library operations to the selected language and retest their guarantees.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Greedy-looking choices fail when future interactions violate the required property. The draft therefore rejects: Choosing the largest immediate reward is always globally optimal.

includes: Make locally optimal choices only when exchange or structural reasoning supports global correctness.
Design a greedy solution, state its ordering and produce a counterexample when the property fails.

Across C, C++, Java, and Python, keep the state transition and invariant language-neutral first; then map indexes, numeric overflow, recursion, mutable collections, hashing, and library operations to the selected language and retest their guarantees.
excludes: Greedy-looking choices fail when future interactions violate the required property.

outcomes: Design a greedy solution, state its ordering and produce a counterexample when the property fails.

examples.title: Greedy choice and counterexamples: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: Interval scheduling selects the compatible interval with earliest finish and proves an exchange with an optimal solution.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: Interval scheduling selects the compatible interval with earliest finish and proves an exchange with an optimal solution.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: A greedy rule is valid only with a correctness argument showing each local choice can belong to an optimal solution.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because A greedy rule is valid only with a correctness argument showing each local choice can belong to an optimal solution.

examples.title: Greedy choice and counterexamples: implementation transfer
examples.situation: Across C, C++, Java, and Python, keep the state transition and invariant language-neutral first; then map indexes, numeric overflow, recursion, mutable collections, hashing, and library operations to the selected language and retest their guarantees.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: Across C, C++, Java, and Python, keep the state transition and invariant language-neutral first; then map indexes, numeric overflow, recursion, mutable collections, hashing, and library operations to the selected language and retest their guarantees.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Greedy-looking choices fail when future interactions violate the required property.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Choosing the largest immediate reward is always globally optimal.
misconceptions.correction: A greedy rule is valid only with a correctness argument showing each local choice can belong to an optimal solution.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: Choosing the largest immediate reward is always globally optimal.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Greedy choice and counterexamples, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.
usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.

recap.summary: You should now be able to design a greedy solution, state its ordering and produce a counterexample when the property fails. Keep this boundary in view: Greedy-looking choices fail when future interactions violate the required property.
